title: dbt at Scale: Managing 500+ Models Without Losing Your Mind
page_content: dbt at Scale: Managing 500+ Models Without Losing Your MindVipra Software Private Limited10 min read·1 hour ago--ListenShareA Banking Data Platform Case Study — S3 → GCS → BigQuery + dbt in ProductionPublication: Vipra Software — Data Engineering Insights Author: Principal Data Architect, Vipra Software Private Limited Original article & full technical reference: https://www.viprasoftware.com/articles/article-dbt-at-scale-banking Tags: dbt data-engineering bigquery google-cloud aws banking data-platform analytics sql pythonThis article was originally published by Vipra Software Private Limited. Full version with interactive diagrams, code annotations, and architecture visuals: 👉 viprasoftware.com/articles/article-dbt-at-scale-bankingThe Situation Nobody Talks AboutYou start with 40 dbt models. It feels clean. You can hold the entire DAG in your head.Then six months later you have 200. A year in, you have 340. Someone has named a model customer_metrics. Someone else has also named a different model customer_metrics — in a different folder — producing different numbers. Your full pipeline run takes 6 hours 20 minutes. A regulatory report is wrong and it takes 4.5 hours just to find the bug.This is the story of how a 12-person data engineering team at a large retail bank went from that chaos to 560+ dbt models running cleanly in 87 minutes — on 620 TB of banking data, with full PII governance, CI/CD, and a compliance team that finally trusted the numbers.Everything in this article is drawn from real production. The numbers are real. The failures are real.Who We AreVipra Software Private Limited is a specialist data engineering and cloud services company with teams across Bengaluru, Dublin, Sydney, Dubai, and Bangkok. We design and operate large-scale data platforms on GCP, AWS, and Azure — for banks, financial institutions, healthcare providers, logistics operators, and global enterprises.This case study comes directly from our production work in financial services data engineering.Before: A Functioning DisasterWhen we inherited this platform, the numbers were stark:Metric State at Handover dbt models 340, across 2 repositories Full pipeline runtime 6 hours 20 minutes Naming conventions 4 conflicting approaches in staging alone Data quality test coverage 12% of models Production incidents/month ~2 (transformation-related) Time to debug a lineage issue 4.5 hours averageThe problems weren’t technical. They were architectural and cultural — the kind that accumulate silently until they become operationally catastrophic.After: 18 Months LaterMetric After Redesign dbt models 560+, clean domain-driven structure Full pipeline runtime 87 minutes Naming conventions 1 enforced standard, CI-validated Data quality test coverage 89% of models Production incidents/month 0 (transformation-related) Monthly BigQuery compute cost $3,100 (down from $8,400)The Architecture: S3 → GCS → BigQuery → dbtSource SystemsOur banking data originated from four categories:Core banking (Oracle): Nightly full extracts as compressed CSVs, delivered to AWS S3 via managed file transfer.Event streams: Internet banking clickstreams, payment initiation events, fraud signals — 4.2 million events/day via Apache Kafka topics.Regulatory feeds: KYC verification results, AML screening outputs, sanctions list match data — pushed by third-party vendors over SFTP.Internal systems: HR, branch performance, product pricing via scheduled API exports.The Ingestion Layer: AWS S3 → Google Cloud StorageWe ran a hybrid multi-cloud ingestion layer — not by choice, but because a cloud migration was still in progress when the data platform was being built. A Python-based AWS Lambda validated file integrity, then triggered Google Cloud Storage Transfer Service to move validated files into GCS landing buckets.Kafka streams were consumed by Google Cloud Dataflow pipelines landing raw JSON into GCS in 15-minute micro-batch windows.Lesson #1: Don’t wait for infrastructure perfection. Build for the reality you have, not the architecture you wish existed.The Transformation Layer: Five Data Zonesdbt sat entirely within BigQuery. We structured the transformation layer across five zones — and made them technical constraints, not conventions:raw_*          →  Exact source copies. No transformations. Not touched by dbt.staging_*      →  Cleaned, typed, renamed. One-to-one with sources. Views.intermediate_* →  Business logic joins. Not exposed to consumers. Ephemeral.mart_*         →  Aggregated, consumption-ready. Incremental tables.reporting_*    →  Finance/regulatory views. Tightly access-controlled.The rule that saved us most: no mart model was permitted to reference another mart model. This single constraint eliminated an entire class of dependency hell.The Breaking Point: When dbt Doesn’t ScaleDAG ExplosionWith 340 models in a single project, the lineage graph was visually unmanageable. The real problem: phantom dependencies — chains of 8–10 hops where 3 would have sufficed. One mart model had 14 upstream dependencies. Six of them existed only because someone had copied an older model without pruning lineage.Runtime: 6 Hours and ClimbingThe 6h 20min full refresh wasn’t a BigQuery performance problem. It was dbt execution design:80+ models running as full-table scans on 100M+ row tablesNo partition pruning. No clustering.Sequential execution of models that could have parallelisedDeveloper Conflict at ScaleWith 11 engineers on a single monorepo, schema.yml merge conflicts were a weekly event. Philosophically: "shared ownership." Operationally: no ownership at all.The Naming AnarchyA quick audit found:stg_, staging_, s_, and unprefixed — all used for staging modelsTwo models named customer_metrics producing different numbersNo documented grain on any fact tableWhen two models with the same name produce different numbers, your analytics team stops trusting the data. Rebuilding that trust takes months.Solution 1: Domain-Driven Structure and Hard Zone BoundariesWe reorganised from a flat technical folder structure into a domain-driven hierarchy:models/├── staging/│   ├── core_banking/      # stg_accounts, stg_transactions│   ├── internet_banking/  # stg_ib_sessions, stg_ib_events│   └── compliance/        # stg_kyc_verifications, stg_aml_alerts├── intermediate/│   ├── payments/│   └── customers/└── marts/    ├── payments/           # fct_daily_payments, dim_payment_channel    ├── accounts/           # fct_daily_balances, dim_account    └── compliance/         # fct_aml_risk_scores, fct_regulatory_positionsEach domain had a designated owning team. Pull requests touching /compliance/ required approval from both an engineer and a compliance data steward — enforced via GitHub CODEOWNERS.We enforced naming conventions as technical constraints in CI:Layer Prefix Example Staging stg_ stg_transactions Intermediate int_ int_customer_metrics Fact tables fct_ fct_daily_balances Dimension tables dim_ dim_account Reporting rpt_ rpt_aml_monthly_summaryA model violating naming conventions failed in CI before reaching code review. The first week: three rejected PRs. After that: nobody forgot.Solution 2: Performance — From 6 Hours to 87 MinutesIncremental Models for Everything Over 10M RowsThe single highest-impact change: converting 23 full-refresh fact models to incremental. Our transactions fact table alone accounted for 2 hours of the original runtime.{{    config(        materialized='incremental',        unique_key='balance_snapshot_id',        incremental_strategy='merge',        partition_by={            "field": "snapshot_date",            "data_type": "date",            "granularity": "day"        },        cluster_by=['account_id', 'product_type'],        on_schema_change='sync_all_columns'    )}}SELECT    {{ dbt_utils.generate_surrogate_key(['account_id', 'snapshot_date']) }} AS balance_snapshot_id,    account_id,    snapshot_date,    closing_balance,    available_balance,    product_type,    currency_codeFROM {{ ref('int_account_daily_position') }}{% if is_incremental() %}WHERE snapshot_date >= (SELECT MAX(snapshot_date) FROM {{ this }}) - INTERVAL 3 DAY{% endif %}The 3-day lookback handled late-arriving corrections from the core banking system without requiring a full refresh.BigQuery Partitioning and ClusteringBefore optimisation: a query scanning fct_daily_payments for a single month processed 340 GB. After partition pruning + clustering: the same query processed 4.2 GB.The pattern applied to all mart models:Partition by date column on every time-dimensioned fact tableCluster by most common filter columns (account_id, customer_id, product_type)Avoid clustering on high-cardinality strings (transaction reference numbers made it worse)Thread TuningDefault dbt threads: 4. We bumped to 16. With a properly pruned DAG and BigQuery’s concurrency capabilities, models that previously ran sequentially now batched in parallel. Runtime reduction from threads alone: ~40 minutes.Solution 3: dbt-osmosis for Schema Management at ScaleBy 300 models, our schema.yml files were in entropy. Outdated descriptions. Missing column tests. Documentation copied from tables that had been refactored away two versions ago.Manual remediation: weeks. dbt-osmosis: mostly automated.What It Did For UsSchema propagation was the killer feature. Add risk_band to stg_accounts — dbt-osmosis automatically propagates that column description to every downstream intermediate and mart model that references it via ref(). Previously: 6–8 manual schema.yml edits per new column.# Sync column descriptions upstream → downstreamdbt-osmosis yaml refactor --project-dir . --profiles-dir .# Reorganise schema files to match folder structure  dbt-osmosis yaml organise --project-dir . --profiles-dir .We had 80 legacy models in a flat /models/legacy/ folder. dbt-osmosis handled all schema.yml restructuring for the migration, saving approximately 3 weeks of manual work.Critical caveat: dbt-osmosis propagates whatever description exists upstream — including wrong ones. Audit your staging model documentation before running a mass propagation. Bad documentation at scale is worse than no documentation at all.Solution 4: CI/CD That Actually Catches ThingsEvery dbt model change went through four CI stages before merging:stages:  - name: Lint and naming check    run: |      dbt debug      python scripts/validate_model_names.py  - name: Compile and parse    run: dbt compile --select state:modified+  - name: Test on modified models    run: |      dbt run --select state:modified+ --target ci      dbt test --select state:modified+ --target ci  - name: Documentation coverage check    run: python scripts/check_doc_coverage.py --min-coverage 80The state:modified+ selector was the critical piece — it runs tests not just on the changed model but on all downstream dependents. A breaking change to stg_transactions triggers tests on every mart and report that depends on it.Tiered Testing StrategyNot all test failures are equal. We formalised this:Tier 1 (every model): not_null + unique on PKs, accepted_values on categoricals. Tier 2 (mart + reporting): Row count ±15% thresholds, referential integrity, freshness checks. Tier 3 (compliance + regulatory): Balance reconciliation ±0.01%, record completeness. Hard blocks. No human override.Solution 5: PII Governance in a Regulated BankThis is where most dbt articles fall short. In banking, PII is a first-class design constraint, not an afterthought.Custom Masking Macros-- In every staging model touching PII:SELECT    customer_id,    {{ mask_pii('first_name') }}         AS first_name,    {{ mask_pii('date_of_birth') }}       AS date_of_birth,    {{ mask_pii('national_id_number') }}  AS national_id_number,    {{ hash_identifier('email_address') }} AS email_hashFROM {{ source('core_banking', 'raw_customers') }}-- macros/mask_pii.sql{% macro mask_pii(column_name) %}    {% if target.name == 'prod' %}        {{ column_name }}    {% else %}        SHA256(CAST({{ column_name }} AS STRING))    {% endif %}{% endmacro %}In non-production targets, PII columns are SHA256-hashed. Production data was never replicated to developer environments. CI ran against a synthetic dataset generated from production schemas with completely fabricated values. A firm rule with no exceptions.BigQuery Role-Based Access ControlTier Datasets Accessible Teams Standard mart_*, reporting_public_* Analytics, Product Elevated + staging_*, intermediate_* Data Engineering Restricted + reporting_compliance_*, reporting_regulatory_* Compliance, Risk, AuditRestricted tier required formal access request, manager approval, and quarterly review — as BigQuery IAM bindings managed in Terraform, not ad-hoc grants.The Hard LessonsWe waited too long to enforce naming conventions. At 100 models it’s an annoyance. At 300 it’s a daily productivity tax on every engineer. Establish and automate enforcement in week one.Ephemeral models are massively underused. 60% of our intermediate models became ephemeral. The DAG became dramatically cleaner overnight, storage costs dropped, and lineage simplified.The monorepo was wrong for us at 300+ models. Splitting into three domain projects (transactional, compliance, customer) with a shared macros package reduced merge conflicts by ~80% and let domain teams deploy independently.Synthetic data for CI is not optional in banking. Three weeks to build. Paid back in audit confidence within the first quarter. In a regulated environment, running CI against real customer data — even in “dev” — is a governance risk.dbt is not an observability solution. dbt tests tell you if the data that arrived passes your rules. They don’t tell you if the data you expected to arrive actually arrived. We built a separate pipeline health monitoring layer (Python + BigQuery metadata tables) for completeness checks.Invest in macros early. We wrote 40+ custom macros: PII masking, audit logging, standard test generators, naming validators. The compound return on upfront macro quality is enormous.Key TakeawaysEnforce naming conventions at 50 models. Feels early. Is exactly right.Make zone boundaries technical constraints, not conventions. If a mart can reference a mart, eventually it will.Convert all large fact tables to incremental immediately. Full refreshes on 100M+ row tables in a daily pipeline are an architectural smell.Partition and cluster everything in BigQuery. 340 GB → 4.2 GB on the same query.Use state:modified+ in CI, always. Testing only changed models and downstream dependents makes CI fast enough to actually be used.Synthetic data for non-production is non-negotiable in regulated industries.Use CODEOWNERS for domain ownership. Shared ownership without accountability is no ownership.Run dbt-osmosis proactively. Not as a rescue tool — as regular maintenance.Tier your data quality tests. A null PK is a hard stop. A 3% volume variance is a warning. Model this explicitly.Monitor data arrival, not just data quality. dbt tests only run on data that arrived.ClosingManaging dbt at scale in a banking environment is fundamentally a people and process problem that technology can only partially solve.The most valuable thing our team did wasn’t a tool or a dbt feature. It was spending two days with all 12 engineers aligning on principles — what models should be named, who owned what, what a test failure meant — before writing a single line of SQL.That alignment, documented and enforced, was the foundation everything else was built on.The cost of addressing these problems at 300 models versus 100 models is roughly 5x. Every month you delay establishing structure is technical debt accumulating at compound interest.Make those decisions early. Your future self — debugging a pipeline at 11pm before a regulatory submission — will be grateful.About Vipra SoftwareVipra Software Private Limited is a specialist data engineering and cloud services company building large-scale data platforms for enterprises across financial services, healthcare, retail, and logistics.We work with organisations across India, Europe, the Middle East, and Asia-Pacific to design, build, and operate data infrastructure on AWS, Google Cloud Platform, and Microsoft Azure.What we do:dbt platform architecture, migration, and governanceBigQuery, Snowflake, and Databricks engineeringReal-time streaming with Apache Kafka and Google DataflowCloud data lakehouse design (AWS, GCP, Azure)PII governance and regulatory data complianceBI and analytics enablement (Looker, Power BI, Tableau)Full article and technical reference: https://www.viprasoftware.com/articles/article-dbt-at-scale-bankingContact us:🌐 viprasoftware.com💼 LinkedIn: Vipra Software📧 info@viprasoftware.comOriginally published by Vipra Software Private Limited.
